issue_body:
Clize is geared towards letting code still be used as code, but the legacy of clize's docstring format impairs this.

Most notably, they cannot be properly parsed by sphinx.ext.autodoc as it is. A sphinx extension could be devised for this. However it is more preferable for Clize to support sphinx/docutils fields since it falls in line with the goal of not needing to learn anything new to use Clize.

Special care needs to be given towards the following:
- Features of Clize docstrings such as customizable ordering and naming sections for named options must be preserved. 
- Decorators must be handled correctly (epsy/sigtools#6)

It could be acceptable to diminish the flexibility of sections to just one per decorator.

Optionally, a directive could be added to format a function to be documented as a command-line interface.

Also, sphinx's contributed napoleon extension could be used to support Google- and Numpy-style docstrings.

In Twisted 16.3 we added `IProtocolNegotiationFactory`, which `TLSMemoryBIOProtocol` uses to determine what protocols (if any) should be negotiated via ALPN/NPN. `TLSMemoryBIOProtocol` does this when `_createConnection` fires, potentially setting callbacks and data on the context returned by calling `.getContext()` on the the connection object from `serverConnectionForTLS`.

Unfortunately, txsni potentially swaps out the TLS contexts midway through the TLS handshake. This means that the callbacks and data that `TLSMemoryBIOProtocol` sets are only ever set on the *default* TLS context: the others, potentially swapped in later in the connection process, do not have this data provided to them.

This means that, in practice, txsni and protocol negotiation do not work together.

_From [almog1...@gmail.com](https://code.google.com/u/106388495642043329325/) on May 21, 2012 05:52:27_

I have been encountered a problem which I found also some people complaining about on the net.

The description on the error from here http://stackoverflow.com/questions/6337878/parsing-pcap-files-with-dpkt-python is about the dpkt.http.parse_headers function
"""
While it seems to effectively parse most of the packets, I'm receiving a NeedData("premature end of headers") exception on some. They appear to be valid packets within WireShark, so I'm a bit confused as to why the exceptions are being thrown.
"""

I investigated the problem and found that the exception is raised if the HTTP is not ending with "\r\n\r\n".

I have researched more than 10 Milion Http Request and there wasn't even one packet where this exception was relevant.

I changed the parse headers on my installatioin to look like this

def parse_headers(f):
    """Return dict of HTTP headers parsed from a file object."""
    d = {}
    while 1:
        line = f.readline()
        if not line:
            break
        \# I commented  this line out because the way this parser works is wrong.
        \# When the http does not finish with '\r\n\r\n' the parsing is faild!
        \#    raise dpkt.NeedData('premature end of headers')

        \# The next two liness are responsibble for stopping the parsing when the
        \# line equals '\r\n'
        line = line.strip()
        if not line:
            break
        l = line.split(None, 1)
        if not l[0].endswith(':'):
            raise dpkt.UnpackError('invalid header: %r' % line)
        k = l[0][:-1].lower()
        v = len(l) != 1 and l[1] or ''
        if k in d:
            if not type(d[k]) is list:
                d[k] = [d[k]]
            d[k].append(v)
        else:
            d[k] = v
    return d


and its working great.

Many thanks,
Almog

issue_title: Exception when hcitool scan result contains invalid characters
issue_body:
We've been using pygatt to assist with development of embedded BLE devices.  Very useful. Thanks.
However, we've encountered a minor issue with a large impact.  Occasionally an incorrectly configured development device will transmit invalid (non Unicode) data in the 'name' field of the hcitool scan response.
This will break the scan functionality of every device using the pygatt GATTTool backend within range of the rogue device.  A Unicode exception is triggered on this line in the scan() method of the GATTToolBackend class in gatttool.py:
   ` for line in scan.before.decode('utf-8').split('\r\n'):`

The local fix we are applying is to modify the line to be:
    `for line in scan.before.decode('utf-8', 'replace').split('\r\n'):`

Issue raised so it could be included in the official source if desired.

issue_title: Checking whether an LDAP entry contains an item raises an LDAPCursorError if the key for the item isn't found
issue_body:
```EntryBase.__contains__``` calls ```EntryBase.__getitem__``` and returns false on an ```LDAPKeyError```.

```__getitem__``` appears to have raised an ```LDAPKeyError``` in 2.2.3 and 2.2.4 if the key wasn't found, but at some point between then and now, that was changed to ```LDAPCursorError``` and ```__contains__``` was not updated to reflect that change. 

This has the result of code checking whether LDAP entries contain items that they don't using 2.2.4 to return False as expected, but to raise an unhandled LDAPCursorError exception with later versions of ldap3, such as 2.4.1.

I'd like to request either that ```LDAPCursorError``` be changed back to ```LDAPKeyError``` there, or if that change was intentional then ```__contains__``` be updated to catch that exception and return False when it does. 

Although probably not the best implementation, I have submitted a proposed solution in https://github.com/cannatag/ldap3/pull/491 which I'm using locally as a patch until an official fix for this is released.

issue_title: ValueError is raised when annotated functions are used
issue_body:
When a method is called with annotated variables, then the following error message is returned:

```org.txdbus.PythonException.ValueError: Function has keyword-only arguments or annotations, use getfullargspec() API which can support them```

This is because getargspec does not support the new signature formats of keyword-only arguments and annotations. This issue is easily fixed:

In file txdbus/objects.py  line 488 replace
```python
    def _set_method_flags(self, method_obj):
        """
        Sets the \"_dbusCaller\" boolean on the \"dbus_*\" methods. This
        is a one-time operation used to flag each method with a boolean
        indicating whether or not they accept the \"dbusCaller\" keyword
        argument
        """
        args = inspect.getargspec(method_obj)[0]
        needs_caller = False

        if len(args) >= 1 and args[-1] == 'dbusCaller':
            needs_caller = True

        method_obj.__func__._dbusCaller = needs_caller
```

by:
```python
    def _set_method_flags(self, method_obj):
        """
        Sets the \"_dbusCaller\" boolean on the \"dbus_*\" methods. This
        is a one-time operation used to flag each method with a boolean
        indicating whether or not they accept the \"dbusCaller\" keyword
        argument
        """
        try:
            args = inspect.getfullargspec(method_obj)[0]
        except AttributeError:
            args = inspect.getargspec(method_obj)[0]
        needs_caller = False

        if len(args) >= 1 and args[-1] == 'dbusCaller':
            needs_caller = True

        method_obj.__func__._dbusCaller = needs_caller
```

This maintains backwards compatibility with python2 and supports the new signatures introduced in python3.

issue_title: import keyring fails with AttributeError when dbus is partially available
issue_body:
I have a Linux environment (Ubuntu Xenial) created with `python3-dbus` (implicitly) installed but also `python3.6` installed from the deadsnakes repo. In this environment, the `dbus` package is available (in /usr/local/lib/python3/dist-packages) but the underlying `_dbus_bindings` is not available (because the bindings were compiled for Python 3.5). In that environment, if one attempts to import dbus, one gets:

```
>>> import dbus
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/dbus/__init__.py", line 82, in <module>
    import dbus.types as types
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/dbus/types.py", line 6, in <module>
    from _dbus_bindings import (
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named '_dbus_bindings'
```

If one attempts to import dbus again, a different error occurs:

```
>>> import dbus
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/dbus/__init__.py", line 81, in <module>
    import dbus.exceptions as exceptions
AttributeError: module 'dbus' has no attribute 'exceptions'
```

And because keyring has two modules each of which attempt to import dbus but trapping only ImportErrors, the import will fail.

```
>>> import keyring
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/dist-packages/keyring/__init__.py", line 3, in <module>
    from .core import (set_keyring, get_keyring, set_password, get_password,
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/dist-packages/keyring/core.py", line 153, in <module>
    init_backend()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/dist-packages/keyring/core.py", line 66, in init_backend
    keyrings = filter(limit, backend.get_all_keyring())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/dist-packages/keyring/util/__init__.py", line 21, in wrapper
    func.always_returns = func(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/dist-packages/keyring/backend.py", line 184, in get_all_keyring
    _load_backends()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/dist-packages/keyring/backend.py", line 142, in _load_backends
    list(map(_load_backend, backends))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/dist-packages/keyring/backend.py", line 133, in _load_backend
    mod = importlib.import_module('.' + name, package)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/importlib/__init__.py", line 126, in import_module
    return _bootstrap._gcd_import(name[level:], package, level)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/dist-packages/keyring/backends/kwallet.py", line 12, in <module>
    import dbus
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/dbus/__init__.py", line 81, in <module>
    import dbus.exceptions as exceptions
AttributeError: module 'dbus' has no attribute 'exceptions'
```

In my opinion, this is an upstream issue in either Ubuntu or dbus, but keyring should probably work around the bug.

There appears no way to remove a key from a keyring.

Setting to None is not accepted (barfs in the C interface) and an empty string is not really a suitable work around.

issue_title: SSH session not active if connecting in a function
issue_body:
Hi, thanks for this great package! I'm having an issue but I'm not sure if the problem is in scp or paramiko, perhaps you have some insight.

First, this works:
```
ssh = paramiko.SSHClient()
ssh.load_system_host_keys()
ssh.connect(hostname=HOST, username=USER, key_filename=KEY)
x = scp.SCPClient(ssh.get_transport())
with x as c:
    print(c)
```

But identical code where I have just moved the connection creation to a separate function doesn't work:
```
def get_client():
    ssh = paramiko.SSHClient()
    ssh.load_system_host_keys()
    ssh.connect(hostname=HOST, username=USER, key_filename=KEY)
    return scp.SCPClient(ssh.get_transport())
with get_client() as c:
    print(c)
```
I get the following error:
```
SSHException: SSH session not active
```

Am I doing something wrong here or is there a bug in scp or paramiko? Thanks for any help!

issue_title: Add ability to search in "Conversations" and in chat tabs
issue_body:
I've been using hangups for the past few months and really like it.

I find that the most important missing feature is being able to search, especially in the "Conversations" tab.  I use hangups with an enterprise google apps account, and have hundreds of Conversations in the "Conversations" tab (at least the last 50 are relevant and need to be used daily).

It's quite tedious to have to search with my eyes the proper row and then scroll down to it, when trying to efficiently send a message.  I would love being able to search by typing something like:
`/searchstring` + [enter]
Which would then put my cursor on the first match, and then typing `n` would go the the next match (vim like bindings).

Being able to search within the chat tabs would be very useful too.

Thanks for all the great work.

document

Steps to reproduce:

[include]
path = /path/to/1

I'd like to add the [DEPLOF](http://mems.technion.ac.il/) font to gdspy.

![4441325253959](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2075083/18014870/41c0ed3a-6b82-11e6-9a60-0d2fa3c6e4ee.gif)


It's a font used on other layout editors (e.g. [LayoutEditor](http://www.layouteditor.net/wiki/Credits) [CleWin](http://www.wieweb.com/nojava/downloadframe.html) ), and is purpose-built for 2D layout/fab.  I received permission from the creator of the MATLAB gdsii-toolbox (Ulf Griesmann) to adapt his implementation, and have it basically ready to go.  My question is whether how I should write the commit--I was thinking of adding an additional argument `font` to the __init__ argument of the Text class.  So that instead of 

`def __init__(self, text, size, position=(0, 0), horizontal=True, angle=0, layer=0, datatype=0)`

it would now be

`def __init__(self, text, size, position=(0, 0), horizontal=True, angle=0, font = 'block', layer=0, datatype=0)`

where `font` could be chosen to be either 'block' (the current font) or 'deplof' (the proposed font), so the change would be transparent and no one's layouts would be changed, but users would now have the option to select the DEPLOF font.  (and perhaps down the road the default could be changed to DEPLOF)

Does sound reasonable?

issue_title: What is the expected behavior for receiver.response_header?
issue_body:
Currently it's confusing.  The specs say "The header is contructed using the same process as the client's request header. The server uses the same credentials and other artifacts provided by the client to constructs the normalized request string. The ext and hash values are replaced with new values based on the server response. The rest as identical to those used by the client."

Receiver.response_header does not return a nonce, id, or timestamp:
```python
>>> receiver.response_header
'Hawk mac="...", hash="..."'
```
Sender.accept_response warns if you do not check for a nonce:
```python
>>> response_content = '{"msg": "Hello, dear friend"}'
>>> response_content_type = 'application/json'
>>> header = receiver.respond(content=response_content, content_type=response_content_type)
>>> response = {
...     'headers': {'Server-Authorization': receiver.response_header,
...                 'Content-Type': response_content_type},
...     'content': response_content
... }
>>> sender.accept_response(
...     response['headers']['Server-Authorization'],
...     content=response['content'],
...     content_type=response['headers']['Content-Type'],
... )
seen_nonce was None; not checking nonce. You may be vulnerable to replay attacks
```
Should the nonce, timestamp, and id be included ("The rest is identical to those used by the client.")?  If so, we should add some failing tests.  If not, the warning message is confusing.

Also see #7.

issue_title: Hotkey combinations with the windows key disable system hotkeys
issue_body:
If a hotkey like `windows+c` is defined with this module, all the other system hotkeys that use the `windows` key (like `windows+e`, `windows+d`, `windows+1` etc.) stop working.

Sample code:

    import keyboard
    keyboard.add_hotkey('windows+c', lambda: print('windows+c'), suppress=True)
    keyboard.wait()

While this program is running, hitting `windows+c` prints `windows+c` as expected, but all the other `windows+` hotkeys don't work anymore.

issue_title: Provide IQ API with strong ordering guarantees
issue_body:
The current IQ API has a main shortcoming: due to its inherent focus on coroutines, the strong ordering guarantees of the XMPP XML Stream get lost a bit. For example, when registering IQ handlers for two different IQs, we cannot say for sure which IQ handler executes first when the two IQs arrive in the same chunk of buffer handed to the XMLStream protocol. Another example are possible races between the return of the ``send`` coroutine function and execution of IQ request handlers.

This has caused quite a bit of headache in the roster and avatar implementations, since they act on rather large state and that state needs to stay in sync with the servers state to be valid.

To solve this, I propose the following API changes:

1. ``register_iq_request_coro`` -> ``register_iq_request_awaitable`` and allow any function which returns an awaitable to be registered. This includes coroutine functions, but also normal functions returning a Future. The result of the future must adhere to the current specifications for IQ request coroutine function results.

    The ``_coro`` version would become an alias to the ``_awaitable`` version, and is deprecated. To be removed in 1.0.

2. ``send`` gets an additional "callback" argument for use with IQ get/set stanzas. Now I’m not 100% sure. Either:

    1. the callback must be a function returning an awaitable. It is executed right when the IQ result is received. The awaitable is awaited by and the result is returned from ``send``. **or**

    2. the callback must be a normal function without specific return type. It is simply executed right when the IQ result is received. It does not influence the result of ``send``.

  In any case, if the callback is omitted, ``send`` behaves as currently. If the ``callback`` is passed for non-IQ-request stanzas, an error is raised.

This way, we will have a strong order guarantee, since those functions will be called right from the stanza processing loop.

Benefits: we should get around quite a bit of locking, e.g. the global avatar lock (#141) and the roster lock, if we play it well.

issue_title: fork example ignores child processes exit code
issue_body:
The fork example (https://github.com/jonashaag/bjoern/blob/master/tests/fork.py) ignores child processes' exit codes. Whatever happens to the children, the parent process will exit gracefully. This does not allow to run it with supervisord or similar means (which automatically restart crashed processes).

Additionally, the fork example will allow a situation when (on a multi-CPU machine) half of child processes crashed for some reason, the other half is still alive, and the server is thus underperforming (not utilizing all CPU cores).

I personally reworked the end of the file like this:

```python
        try:
            # Wait the for first worker to exit. They should never exit!
            # Once first is dead, kill the others and exit with error code.
            pid, xx = os.wait()
            worker_pids.remove(pid)
        finally:
            for pid in worker_pids:
                os.kill(pid, signal.SIGINT)
            exit(1)
```

issue_title: create_invite doesn't accept Server as destination
issue_body:
[The document](http://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#discord.Client.create_invite) says 

```
create_invite(destination, **options)
Creates an invite for the destination which could be either a Server or Channel.
```

but when i try to create an invite with Server as destination, i'm getting `NOT FOUND (status code: 404): Unknown Channel` error.

`Client.create_invite` calls `self.http.create_invite` with this line:

```r = Route('POST', '/channels/{channel_id}/invites', channel_id=channel_id)```

It's obvious that, the call is not for using with Server. Also, in [HTTP API's  doc](https://discordapp.com/developers/docs/resources/channel#create-channel-invite), there isn't any mention to server for that method and there is no other method to create an invitation for a server.

There are to possible solutions:
* Fixing the misleading documentation
* If the parameter is server, creating an invitation for it's main channel.

I'd prefer second one.

`user.block()` `user.send_friend_request()` does nothing for me :/

```py
    @commands.command(pass_context=True)
    async def block(self, ctx, *, user: discord.User=None):
        if not user and isinstance(ctx.channel, discord.DMChannel):
            user = ctx.channel.recipient
        await ctx.message.edit(content=self.bot.bot_prefix+'Blocking %s'%user.mention)
        await user.block()
```
```py
    @commands.command(pass_context=True)
    async def friend(self, ctx, *, user: discord.User=None):
        if not user and isinstance(ctx.channel, discord.DMChannel):
            user = ctx.channel.recipient
        await ctx.message.edit(content=self.bot.bot_prefix+'Sending friend request to %s'%user.mention)
        await user.send_friend_request()
```

Am i using them wrong or is it really bugged out?

a = ('a',)

issue_title: KafkaConsumer pattern on newly created topic
issue_body:
Doing this works well on existing topics (let say 'crawl.1.fetched' and 'crawl.2.fetched'):
```
self.consumer = KafkaConsumer(bootstrap_servers="kafka:9092", group_id=self.group_id, auto_offset_reset='earliest')
self.consumer.subscribe(pattern="crawl.*.fetched")
```
if I create a third one 'crawl.3.fetch' and I am expecting the KafkaConsumer to start processing this new topic but it doesn't. If I restart the application of course "crawl.3.fetch" get processed.

---

If I use a topic pattern with the kafka console consumer tool, it is working as expected
```
/opt/kafka/bin/kafka-console-consumer.sh --zookeeper zookeeper:2181 --topic crawl.*.fetched --from-beginning
```
This consumer will process any new topic created with the pattern crawl.*.fetched.

---

issue_title: add new public method get_partitions_count to class KafkaConsumer
issue_body:
A method get_partitions_count would be helpful when I use multiple message handlers to process messages from one consumer.

I use produce(topic1,key1,msg) to make sure all messages with key1 go to the same partition, and want to dispatch messages from consumer(topic1,group1) to multiple message handlers, one handler process one partition in order to preserve the message insertion order.

### Pyseudocode
```python
consumer=KafkaConsumer(topic1, bootrap, group1)
count = consumer.get_partitions_count()
handlers=[Handler() for i in range(count)]

def dispatch(msg):
    handlers [msg.partition].handle(msg)
```

issue_title: KafkaConsumer does not revoke deleted partitions when using a ConsumerRebalanceListener
issue_body:
In my system, a KafkaConsumer uses a regex subscription to subscribe to topics in the form of `<user_id>.<topic>`. A `ConsumberRebalanceListener` is used to write topic offsets and the aggregated topic states into a local cache. Automated tests create and delete users (i.e. topics).

When new topics are created, everything works fine. The logs show that the subscription changed, currently subscribed partitions are revoked and the partitions of the created topics are added to the set of previously assigned TopicsPartitions.

When a topic is deleted (via a `DeleteTopicsRequest`), the log shows that the subscription changes ("Updatding subscribed topics to: …"). The subscription no longer contains the deleted topics, which is correct. Afterwards the currently assigned partitions are revoked in `ConsumerCoordinator._on_join_prepare` ("Revoking previously assigned partitions …"). However, the set of revoked partitions no longer contains the partitions of the deleted topic, because the subscription was already updated.

I would expect that deleted TopicPartitions are also passed to `ConsumerRebalanceListener.on_partitions_revoked`. Is my expectation wrong or is this a bug?

issue_title: KafkaConsumer Re-process messages / rewind to offset not working as expected
issue_body:
Trying to re-process messages in a partitioned topic by ```seek(p, offset)```. To do that, first I need to get the active assignment which only works after a successful ```poll()```. I assume if I specify ```auto_offset_reset``` to ```'earliest'```, I should be able to do that, but it didn't work. The code is here:

```
from kafka import KafkaConsumer

consumer = KafkaConsumer('my.topic', group_id='grp001', 
                         bootstrap_servers='localhost:9092', 
                         auto_offset_reset='earliest')

while True: 
    rec = consumer.poll(timeout_ms=1000, max_records=1)
    if len(rec) > 0:
        break;

topic_partitions = consumer.assignment()
offset = dict(zip(topic_partitions, [1503500000000]*len(topic_partitions)))
for p in topic_partitions:
    consumer.seek(p, offset[p].offset)
```

The call to ```poll()``` never succeeds until new messages are produced. Am I missing anything? I thought setting ```auto_offset_reset``` to ```'earliest'``` would let me go back and re-process the old messages. Do I need to do anything else? 

Running kafka-python 1.3.4 and kafka 0.11.0. The topic has three partitions. Thanks for any help.

issue_title: Handle temporary errors on getting offsets for consumer pending request
issue_body:
There are several very common [Kafka errors](https://github.com/mumrah/kafka-python/blob/4b064340e8796cb85307e014853ddb0435809f2f/kafka/client.py#L252) related with cluster changes. Currently these errors are followed by resetting metadata operation but then they are reraised as we're not sure if it's temporary or not.

But afaik **NotLeaderForPartitionError** is temporary, and in the case of getting offsets for calculating pending messages count - the offset request should be retried after getting fresh metadata. Is it so or it's a responsibility of user application and not kafka-python library to handle such errors when get pending count?

Imagine we want to use bitstruct for a very large amount of data that we want to process and convert to python types. This would involve many (in my case on the order of 1e9) calls to unpack and/or pack function. In this function it alsways parses the fmt string. I would propose to put these functions into a class and therefore give the ability to store i.e. the result of the fmt string parsing for subsequent calls with new data. This would definitely save quite some time.

If you agree with this idea to change it into a class maybe to allow a more stateful approach, I would try to do it and make a PR.

Best,
dreichler

In my opinion not only the Python object API has to be sane for humans but also (and maybe even more) the error messages.

There is this `validate_type_mayadt` decorator which throws a `ValueError` exception if the given arguments are not of type `MayaDT` with a somehow insufficient error message. It'd be great if it would actually tell us which operation failed because of the type mismatch. 

Which operation failed?
```python
assert (dt1 == dt2) >= 1
```

I see multiple solutions here:

1. pass a decorator argument describing the operation: `@validate('less than')`
2. get the function name from the `func` object within the decorator: `func.__name__`
3. ...

However, point 2 leads to an `unhumanized` error message because `__lt__` would be printed - yes we could argue this exception only occurs due to programming error - It'd favor the first one though.

What do you think?

And the resulting exception should be of type [TypeError](https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#TypeError)
See https://github.com/rdaly525/coreir/pull/213

The documentation says, under "things that don't work":

> sys.settrace is the Python feature that coverage.py uses to see what’s happening in your program. If another part of your program is using sys.settrace, then it will conflict with coverage.py, and it won’t be measured properly.

And this is of course correct. That said... Is there a way to both do coverage measurement and also perform some package-specific tracing at the same time? In my case, I have a module which needs to do some tracing for its functionality, and I'd also like to have it (not to mention applications using it) be able to measure coverage.

I tried to naively do something along the following lines:

```python
prev_tracefunc = sys.settrace(my_tracefunc)
... run some code with my tracing ...
sys.settrace(prev_tracefunc)

def my_tracefunc(...):
    ... do my stuff ...
    if prev_tracefunc is not None:
        return prev_tracefunc(...)
    return None
```

However, when running the code under `coverage` (either directly or using `nose`), I discovered that `prev_tracefunc` was still `None`; in addition, while my code run and passed its tests, coverage was not collected for the relevant parts - as per the documentation, but still surprising.

So:

* Is there any magic incantation I could place in my code which would allow it to live peacefully with `coverage.py`?

* If not, would it be possible to tweak `coverage.py` so that such an incantation would become possible?
Ability to hide interfaces in "down" state would be nice. No traffic there anyway.

issue_title: more intelligent setting of k bounds
issue_body:
At this point, the bounds of k are set statically by the user, and by default are quite conservative. 

The default suggests that the typical application requires that k-integrals approximate (0,\infty) [though they are left free for other applications which approximate finite box size etc.].

Given that the requirement is an approximation for a given (set of) integral(s), it would perhaps be more useful to regard k-bounds as being set dynamically from a given user expectation of accuracy at given masses. This would allow the most efficient gridding, and less confusion.

Here's the helper function I wrote:
```
from purl import URL
from urllib import parse

def resolve(base, url):
    """
    Resolves a target URL relative to a base URL in a manner similar to that of a Web browser resolving an anchor tag HREF
    :param base: str|URL
    :param url: str|URL
    :return: URL
    """
    if isinstance(base, URL):
        baseurl = base
    else:
        baseurl = URL(base)

    if isinstance(url, URL):
        relurl = url
    else:
        relurl = URL(url)

    if relurl.host():
        return relurl

    if relurl.path():
        return URL(
            scheme=baseurl.scheme(),
            host=baseurl.host(),
            port=baseurl.port(),
            path=parse.urljoin(baseurl.path(), relurl.path()),
            query=relurl.query(),
            fragment=relurl.fragment(),
        )
    elif relurl.query() or '?' in url:
        return URL(
            scheme=baseurl.scheme(),
            host=baseurl.host(),
            port=baseurl.port(),
            path=baseurl.path(),
            query=relurl.query(),
            fragment=relurl.fragment(),
        )
    elif relurl.fragment() or '#' in url:
        return URL(
            scheme=baseurl.scheme(),
            host=baseurl.host(),
            port=baseurl.port(),
            path=baseurl.path(),
            query=baseurl.query(),
            fragment=relurl.fragment(),
        )
    return baseurl
```

Usage:
```
>>> base = URL('http://user:pass@google.com:8080/path/to/some/doc.html?q=query#frag')
...
>>> print(resolve(base, '../home'))
http://user:pass@google.com:8080/path/to/home

>>> print(resolve(base, 'doc2.html'))
http://user:pass@google.com:8080/path/to/some/doc2.html

>>> print(resolve(base, '?'))
http://user:pass@google.com:8080/path/to/some/doc.html

>>> print(resolve(base, '?q=git'))
http://user:pass@google.com:8080/path/to/some/doc.html?q=git

>>> print(resolve(base, '#'))
http://user:pass@google.com:8080/path/to/some/doc.html?q=query
```

issue_title: Memory Usage for Lots of Unit Tests
issue_body: When a unit test completes, the output and other file representations that `fortpy` constructs to verify the test are still in memory. If you run lots of cases of lots of tests all in a row, it can take a lot of memory. We need to force garbage collection after each test that completes so that this is not a problem. At the very least, we ought to take the file representations out of scope once we have the comparison reports and aggregate statistics generated.

issue_title: EPIC: Improve testing and documentation infrastructure
issue_body:
mtools could benefit from a more versatile testing framework, a standardized coding style, and more accessible documentation. To achieve these goals, I suggest using the following tools:

#### tox
- [tox](https://tox.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) provides automated, standardized testing.
- makes testing against multiple Python versions straightforward, which is a prerequisite for completing #527
- enables test environments for multiple targets (docs, coding style, nosetests)
- enables easy integration of tests and documentation builds with Travis

#### flake8
- [flake8](http://flake8.pycqa.org/en/latest/) includes lint checks provided by the PyFlakes project, PEP8 inspired style checks provided by the PyCodeStyle project, and McCabe complexity checking provided by the McCabe project
- extensible through plugins

#### isort
- [isort](https://readthedocs.org/projects/isort/) checks for standardized import order and style

#### pep257
- [pep257](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pep257) checks that code complies with the docstring conventions suggested in PEP257

#### Sphinx
- [Sphinx](http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/stable/rest.html) is the de facto standard for creating Python documentation with reStructuredText
- integrates cleanly with tox
- style is enforced with doc8
- spelling is checked with sphinxcontrib-spelling
- built documentation can be automatically deployed by Travis to a gh-page

### Implementation steps

- [x] enable tox
  - remove current test lines from setup.py
  - add tox.ini
  - add test-requirements.txt
  - add coverage report
  - add flake8 with pep8-naming, isort, and pep257 as testenvs
  - update .travis.yml
- [x] fix errors found by flake8
- [x] fix errors found by isort
- [x] fix errors found by pep257
- [ ] add flake8, isort, pep257 to full test run
- [x] add RST documentation project with tests
  - include all content from current wiki pages
  - enhance section on contributing (update how to run tests)
  - add section on coding standards
- [ ] add Travis gh-pages deploy script for documentation
- [x] update `README.rst` and repository landing page with links to gh-pages documentation
- [x] remove wiki pages

TtHFanfic.org Fetch URLs not retrieving all stories from page
issue_body:
TtH by default restricts story rating to FR18.

if is_adult is false, then the current behavior is IMO correct.

In this case, I have set is_adult to true, and also have provided login credentials. My TtH account's default rating is further set to FR21.

Download works fine for stories rated FR21 when credentials are provided, but when fetching URLs from a page, it appears that the user is not logged in and the default story rating restriction of FR18 applies, therefore not all stories are downloaded.

is_adult is set to true globally in this case; if it was false I would expect the current behavior.

Example:
For https://www.tthfanfic.org/AuthorStories-20566/Katrina.htm
Expected output (all ratings):
```
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-29997-9/Katrina+BTVS+Builders+Junkyard+Dog.htm
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-29830-4/Katrina+BTVS+Builders.htm
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-28886/Katrina+Art+and+Banners+and+Such.htm
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-27523/Katrina+BTVS+AB+It+s+not+a+case+of+But.htm
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-26962/Katrina+TDWP+BTVS+On+a+Wing+and+a+Prayer.htm
```
Actual output (only FR18 and below):
```
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-29997-9/Katrina+BTVS+Builders+Junkyard+Dog.htm
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-29830-4/Katrina+BTVS+Builders.htm
https://www.tthfanfic.org/Story-27523/Katrina+BTVS+AB+It+s+not+a+case+of+But.htm
```

Note that there is a special case in the code for AO3 to log in because AO3 does not show adult stories without it; that is not exactly the case for TtH, but I've described my findings below.

There does seem to be a session cookie, so if a session can be opened for fetching URLs, then fetching this URL should set the max rating to FR21:
https://www.tthfanfic.org/setmaxrating.php?sitemaxrating=5
After that point, the original page could be fetched.

There is not a specific cookie that saves the default rating.

The other option, of course, is simply to log in prior to fetching, as is done for AO3, in which case the account's specified default maximum rating would apply.

During my tests around response segmentation, I saw error in response format:
1. No one of response or request were segmented.
   This first issue is in my server implementation. I did not have instantiated an `deviceInfoCache` and provided it to `BIP*Application` initialization. So when bacpypes library was trying to send the response, no segmentation data was available.

   Do you confirm this object is mandatory for segmentation processing? In my first understanding, this object is optionnal because most of important segmentation data are provided in request header.

2. Segmentation informations provided in request header are not taken into account.
   The `DeviceInfoCache` is updated with each `iAm` message received to register segmentation information. But I saw an issue when the segmentation requirement from `iAm` message and from request header are not the same. The `iAm` content is always used and request header seems ignored.

   After some research I found in `ServerSSM` implementation two debug logs:
`- update maximum segments accepted?`
`- update maximum max APDU length accepted?`

   When I complete this function with update of `deviceInfoCache` using request header data, the segmentation is done as expected. I'm not totally sure it is the good solution but in current implementation the request header is no more available when the response is segmented.

3. When number of segment required is bigger than max accepted, the segments were sent.
   The last issue appeared when the response size is bigger than the allowed size regarding apdu length and max segments accepted. For example with a response of 150 octets, if client has maxApduLength = 50 and maxSegmentAccepted = 2. The expected result is `ABORT` message with reason `bufferOverflow`.

   But when I try it, 3 segments are received.
   After investigation, I finally found that this case is not checked in `confirmation` function of `SSMServer` class.
   I just added a check if number of required segment is bigger than client max accepted.

4. BTL specific implementation
   This is not an error in library implementation but it is a requirement from BTL implementation guideline (http://c.ymcdn.com/sites/www.bacnetlabs.org/resource/resmgr/Files/BTL_Implementation_Guideline.pdf) regarding segmentation parameter management. When the max segments accepted parameter is not provided, BTL requires to replace it by 2.

   I suggest you to add it in library implementation because it doesn't contradict the standard.

You can find my patch in attachment for more details.
[0018_Segmentation.txt](https://github.com/JoelBender/bacpypes/files/1731831/0018_Segmentation.txt)

Add Interface_Value Property

http://www.bacnet.org/Addenda/Add-135-2012-an-at-au-av-aw-ax-az-Final.pdf

issue_title: Work around guess_type() bug with query strings.
issue_body:
In parse_media_file() a HTTP url gets fed through basename to get the "filename" and then passed to mimetypes.guess_type() to guess what the MIME type is based on the extension of the file. If your file has a query string guess_type() can't figure out the MIME type and returns None. This gets passed up to Twitter which ends up returning an error 'media type unrecognized'.

In some cases you can strip the query string/fragment with urlparse and hand that to python-twitter which will let it work properly. But what about URLs that *require* a query string to work? If you take it off yourself it'll guess the MIME type right but http_to_file() will fail as it's returning bad content. 

As a solution guess_type() should strip query strings before passing the URL to basename on line 218 in twitter_utils.py. The URL should be passed to http_to_file without change.

issue_title: Incorrect use of SSL_write leads to OpenSSL.SSL.Error exception
issue_body:
See horazont/aioxmpp#116. @jomag reported:
```
[3286.014] ERR:  [asyncio] Fatal write error on STARTTLS transport
protocol: <aioxmpp.protocol.XMLStream object at 0xb41d0ad0>
transport: <aioopenssl.STARTTLSTransport object at 0xb41d0c10>Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aioopenssl/__init__.py", line 478, in _write_ready
    nsent = self._sock.send(bytes(self._buffer))
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/site-packages/OpenSSL/SSL.py", line 1254, in send
    self._raise_ssl_error(self._ssl, result)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/site-packages/OpenSSL/SSL.py", line 1172, in _raise_ssl_error
    _raise_current_error()
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/site-packages/OpenSSL/_util.py", line 48, in exception_from_error_queue
    raise exception_type(errors)
OpenSSL.SSL.Error: [('SSL routines', 'ssl3_write_pending', 'bad write retry')]
```

[Reseraching the error condition](https://stackoverflow.com/a/3003410/1248008), it appears that OpenSSL by default does not allow you to change the buffer pointer or size between SSL_write calls which returned with a "NEED_READ" or "NEED_WRITE" condition.

issue_title: How to call the final_check() to check a rule before inserting
Password & Username verification should use regex
issue_body:
So, I was randomly poking around in `Flask-User` today, curious about how passwords were verified & checked. I was really surprised when I found [the code](https://github.com/lingthio/Flask-User/blob/v0.6/flask_user/forms.py#L25). What is currently being done to validate passwords and usernames is very inefficient & unnecessary. Regex is a perfect tool for validating passwords, usernames, emails, and a number of other string- and pattern-based rules.

I believe `Flask-User` is in the midst of active `0.9` development, and thought I'd just drop in a quick note that this could be drastically improved with regex.

For example, password/username validation can be simplified to something like this and still match the same rules:

```python
from re import compile

PASSWORD_REGEX = compile(r'\A(?=.*?\d)(?=.*?[A-Z])(?=.*?[a-z])(?=\S+$).{6,}\Z')
USERNAME_REGEX = compile(r'\A[\w\-\.]{3,}\Z')

def password_is_valid(password):
    return PASSWORD_REGEX.match(password) is not None

def username_is_valid(username):
    return USERNAME_REGEX.match(username) is not None
```

Just thought I'd bring it up for consideration. This really is the perfect use case for regex, and it's pretty odd to see passwords and usernames being validated by iterating over the entire string in Python code, counting how many times each character appears, and then checking those counts.

issue_title: Remove (most) mock objects from whitelist files
issue_body:
We currently mock all objects in our whitelist files, e.g. `whitelist_defaultdict = Whitelist(); whitelist_defaultdict.default_factory`. This has the drawback that we can't test whether `default_factory` really exists (and is not misspelled or missing from the stdlib in this Python version). For non-dynamic objects, we should therefore access the objects directly instead of their mocks. The only place where we have to keep mock objects is the whitelist for the `ast` module, since these methods are created dynamically. To allow importing `collections` in the `collections.py` whitelist file, we have to rename it to `collections_whitelist.py` to avoid name clashes. Here is a diff that shows the necessary changes for `collections.py`:

```
diff --git a/vulture/core.py b/vulture/core.py
index 5400ae6..4f43e80 100644
--- a/vulture/core.py
+++ b/vulture/core.py
@@ -218,7 +218,7 @@ class Vulture(ast.NodeVisitor):
 
         unique_imports = set(item.name for item in self.defined_imports)
         for import_name in unique_imports:
-            path = os.path.join('whitelists', import_name) + '.py'
+            path = os.path.join('whitelists', import_name) + '_whitelist.py'
             if exclude_file(path):
                 self._log('Excluded whitelist:', path)
             else:
diff --git a/vulture/whitelists/collections_whitelist.py b/vulture/whitelists/collections_whitelist.py
index 4af0cdd..c43bc58 100644
--- a/vulture/whitelists/collections_whitelist.py
+++ b/vulture/whitelists/collections_whitelist.py
@@ -1,5 +1,4 @@
-from whitelist_utils import Whitelist
+import collections
 
 # To free memory, the "default_factory" attribute can be set to None.
-whitelist_defaultdict = Whitelist()
-whitelist_defaultdict.default_factory
+collections.defaultdict.default_factory
```

vulture currently detects the case when a `else` block is associated with an `if True` statement, but does not do so in the case of a `while` loop.

For example, the following example wouldn't be detected as unused code:
```py
while True:
    print("Hi")
else:
    # Dead code
    print("Dead code")
```

issue_title: Forward slashes in number formats should be escaped

I've noticed that, at least for rubycoin the get_merkle command fails, I traced this to the following snippet:
```
   async def deserialised_block(self, hex_hash):
        '''Return the deserialised block with the given hex hash.'''
        return await self._send_single('getblock', (hex_hash, True))
```
the second parameter, True, tells the daemon to return a list of dicts representing deserialized tx's rather than simply a list of tx hashes. Im not sure if changing this would break things for other coins so I propose the following solution in controller.py:
```
    async def tx_merkle(self, tx_hash, height):
        '''tx_hash is a hex string.'''
        hex_hashes = await self.daemon_request('block_hex_hashes', height, 1)
        block = await self.daemon_request('deserialised_block', hex_hashes[0])
        tx_hashes = [tx['txid'] for tx in block['tx']] if (isinstance(block['tx'][0], dict) and 'txid' in block['tx'][0].keys()) else block['tx']

        try:
            pos = tx_hashes.index(tx_hash)
        except ValueError:
            raise RPCError('tx hash {} not in block {} at height {:,d}'
                           .format(tx_hash, hex_hashes[0], height))

        idx = pos
        hashes = [hex_str_to_hash(txh) for txh in tx_hashes]
        merkle_branch = []
        while len(hashes) > 1:
            if len(hashes) & 1:
                hashes.append(hashes[-1])
            idx = idx - 1 if (idx & 1) else idx + 1
            merkle_branch.append(hash_to_str(hashes[idx]))
            idx //= 2
            hashes = [double_sha256(hashes[n] + hashes[n + 1])
                      for n in range(0, len(hashes), 2)]

        return {"block_height": height, "merkle": merkle_branch, "pos": pos}
```
rather than assigning block['tx'] to tx_hashes it first checks whether it is a dict or a list, and handles it accordingly. this should maintain compatability with any other daemons. * Generate some static files using webpack
* Load a page in the Flask app
* Make a change to one of the source files and recompile
* Reload the page

Python 3.6.3
Flask==0.12.2
Flask-Webpack==0.1.0

```python

How can I use hashed image files fom within CSS? Do I need to setup Jinja2 in some way that it also processes CSS, because that would mean quite a bit of overhead. 
What is the recommended way to do this?

issue_title: Using eqref instead of ref for latex output
issue_body:
Would it be possible to have an option to use \eqref instead of \ref for latex outputs?

\eqref puts parentheses around the equation number, e.g. `eq. (1)` instead of `eq. 1`.

It might also be nice to reproduce this in html if the option is enabled, so the documents are more uniform across formats.

I read pandoc_eqnos.py and I see no obvious way to do so, but I'm not used to pandocfilters and pandocxnos. If someone would like to implement this, or point me towards the right way to do so, I'd appreciate it a lot.

Thank you!

For reference, here's the relevant code piece that includes the profiler:
```python
from DmpWorkflow.config.defaults import cfg
import flask_profiler
from DmpWorkflow import version
from socket import getfqdn
kind = cfg.get("global", "installation")

if kind == 'server':    
    from flask import Flask
    from flask.ext.mongoengine import MongoEngine
    app = Flask(__name__)
    app.config.update(LOGGER_NAME="core")
    app.config['MONGODB_DB'] = cfg.get("database", "name")
    app.config['MONGODB_USERNAME'] = cfg.get("database", "user")
    app.config['MONGODB_PASSWORD'] = cfg.get("database", "password")
    app.config['MONGODB_HOST'] = cfg.get("database", "host")

        # Prevents circular imports
        from DmpWorkflow.core.views import jobs

        app.register_blueprint(jobs)
        app.register_blueprint(admin)
    
        if app.config['flask_profiler']['enabled']: app.logger.info("started flask profiler")

Since some API calls can be cached, it would be nice if ratelimit could somehow be made aware of the cache.

For example, with the very simple to use [requestss_cache](https://github.com/reclosedev/requests-cache) library you can cache all responses made by the requests library by default. 

If your request is `r = requests.get('http://example.com/api')` you can check `r.from_cache` which will return True if the response was read from cache.

It would be awesome if I could easily make ratelimit aware of the last time an actual API call was made using `r.from_cache` in my function so it would only rate limit actual API calls. Something like this:

```python
@ratelimit(1, 10)
def apiCall():
    r = requests.get('http://example.com/api')
    if r.from_cache:
        ratelimit.skip()
```

Would this be terribly difficult to implement or is there already a way to do this that isn't documented in the readme?

issue_title: wormhole leaks information about failed transfers
issue_body:
If a transfer fails on the receiver side, the sender currently sees an exception explaining what happened on the receiver side.  For instance, if the transfer failed because the receiver already had a file with the same name, the sender sees an exception (with traceback) and a message that states the receiver already had a file by that name:

```
servo:~/tmp/cdtemp.RGVnNd 0$ wormhole send foo
Sending 0 byte file named 'foo'
On the other computer, please run: wormhole receive
Wormhole code is: 3-cherokee-stormy

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/wormhole/cli/cli.py", line 99, in _dispatch_command
    yield maybeDeferred(command)
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/twisted/internet/defer.py", line 1126, in _inlineCallbacks
    result = result.throwExceptionIntoGenerator(g)
 File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/twisted/python/failure.py", line 389, in throwExceptionIntoGenerator
    return g.throw(self.type, self.value, self.tb)
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/wormhole/cli/cmd_send.py", line 53, in go
    yield d
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/twisted/internet/defer.py", line 1128, in _inlineCallbacks
    result = g.send(result)
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/wormhole/cli/cmd_send.py", line 140, in _go
    % them_d["error"])
wormhole.errors.TransferError: remote error, transfer abandoned: file already exists
ERROR: remote error, transfer abandoned: file already exists`
```

Beyond the fact that it would be good if this exception was caught and presented to the user in a straightforward way, the actual exception message is an unnecessary leak of information about the state of the receiver to the sender.  Clearly the protocol has some way of reporting errors from receiver to sender.  However, I'm not sure that there is really any reason that the sender needs to know why the transfer failed, other than there was some problem on the receiver side.  As receiver, I don't necessarily want the sender to know anything about the state of my machine, such as knowing the names of files in my receiving directory.

I guess the point is that I don't think the receiver should send any information back to the sender giving any information about the state of the receivers system.  It should be sufficient just to say that the transfer failed on the receiver side, with no other context.  Sender and receiver can then communicate out-of-band about the failure if they wish.

Also, users should not ever see exception tracebacks for "normal" failure modes.  This type of failure should just print a message to the console ("transfer failed due to receiver error") and wormhole should exit with non-zero return code.

This is probably far-fetched, but wormhole seems like an interesting base for this idea:

Rather than 1-to-1, could we efficiently do 1-to-many transfer of a file - possibly with torrenting for extra efficiency?

I'm in a conference tutorial, where ~200 people are trying to download the same tutorial materials over wifi at once, and soon the download speeds make dial-up look good. We resorted to passing around USB sticks for some files this morning. Surely, in the 21st century, we can do better than this!

Bittorrent would probably work, but half the room would first need to download multi-MB torrent clients, set them up on a range of different platforms, then work out how to start the torrent. Assuming people have Python (OK, I'm at a Python conference :-), wormhole is a couple of instructions that are the same on any platform:

```
pip install magic-wormhole
wormhole recv 8-foo-bar
```

Today I was talking with some folks about DoS protection for an application-specific rendezvous server. One plan we came up with looks like this:

* add a flag to the server that ignores the `list` command (#53)
  * post-0.9.2 clients will tolerate this just fine
  * I'll have to make sure 0.9.2 clients don't block or something
  * we could probably have the `list` command return an empty list to maintain compatibility with older versions)
* add a server setting to control the *minimum* length of an allocated nameplate
* add another server flag that makes it reject a `claim` of any nameplate that wasn't previously created by an `allocate` command
* if anyone *does* try to claim a non-existent nameplate, treat this as evidence of an attack (especially if the server was completely idle at that point, with no active nameplates), and then sinkhole that IP address for some amount of time
* maybe add a way to configure which IP addresses are allowed to use `allocate`, or require a secret token to be included in the `allocate` request, or require a token to use `claim` on a non-allocated nameplate

The overall idea was to support a service which provisions some client application, in which the code-generating client is always run by the same admin (probably on the same machine) as the rendezvous server. This mode would never use "out-of-band" client-generated nameplates (only server-allocated nameplates).

An external attacker would be limited to guessing *when* a wormhole setup might be in progress, guessing *which* nameplate it's using, and then running the protocol as the receiving party. If the nameplate-space is large enough (say at least three digits), and the server is not heavily used, then the attacker has a very high chance of guessing wrong, and claiming a non-existent nameplate. When the server sees this, it is either a failed attack, or a client which mistyped the nameplate digits (and if the server is completely idle, then *any* claim is almost certainly an attack). If we have a high probability of detecting an attack, then rate-limiting or completely blocking requests from that IP address can help raise the cost for the attacker.

(This might help slow down a non-distributed DoS attack. A properly distributed one, where the attacker can make or fake connections from thousands of IP addresses, is not something this will help with)

In one implementation of this, the provisioning "client" has the secret token that allows it (and nobody else) to use the `allocate` API, and the server has a minimum-nameplate-length setting. In another form, nobody uses `allocate`, and instead the provisioning client chooses the (longer) nameplates themselves, and they get a secret token that allows them to `claim` a non-existing nameplate (so from the server it looks just like an out-of-band code selection, except that the codes are really only generated by a single client all the time).

So we need to:

* implement the disable-`list` option (#53)
* either:
  * add server-side minimum-nameplate-length control
  * add flag to reject `claim` of non-allocated nameplates
  * add secret token protection for `allocate`
* or:
  * add flag to disable `allocate`
  * add secret token protection for `claim` of non-allocated nameplates
* and add a hook (subclass of Rendezvous?) to react to rejected `claim` calls

issue_title: **kwargs usage may result in typos in keyword arguments being quietly ignored
issue_body:
`DnsRequest.from_config().get_passive_dns(query='netflix.com', source=['riskiq'])`

When a similar request is made, it goes through fine but source is mistakenly used instead of sources, so the keyword is ignored and data from all sources is returned.

Possible enhancement might be to either rewrite with keyword arguments explicitly stated in function definition, or kwargs checked against all possible keywords, erroring if extras exist.

Also possible to close and ignore and just make sure the documentation is very clear.

issue_title: Convert documentation to reStructuredText, build using Sphinx
issue_body: To be more compatible with readthedocs.io

Currently pyramid_swagger will register the api-docs/* endpoints automatically, with no opportunity for customization. There are several valid reasons to want to customize this (service of a different endpoint, authorization, etc.) and they should be exposed to users.

The implementation we decide on should ideally:
* Involve no configuration for users wanting the default behavior
* Allow those who need to customize full ability. Probably just delegate the entire registration process to them.

Worth noting that this will most likely end with us exposing (for the first time) our internal representation and access methods for the schemas to our users. Thar be dragons.

issue_title: imagehash from memory object saves corrupted file
issue_body:
When I run imagehash against an upload that exists in memory but has not yet been written to disk, the resulting file is corrupted after disk write. For example:

`f = form.image.data`
`filename = secure_filename(f.filename)`
`imghash = imagehash.dhash(Image.open(f))`
`f.save(os.path.join(imgdir, filename))`

The saved file is missing bytes and isn't a valid image.

issue_title: Add support for the 'Infrequent Access' S3 Storage Class
issue_body:
Currently, django-storages supports the STANDARD storage class and the REDUCED_REDUNDANCY storage class.

Amazon appear to be phasing out REDUCED_REDUNDANCY, and in fact, it's more expensive now than STANDARD.

There's a new class, "infrequent access" (`STANDARD_IA`) for which we should add support.

This'll be easy in `s3boto3.py`, but it might be trickier with `s3boto.py`
Any file uploaded to S3 using django-storages grants permission to everyone access to the file--is there a method to not alter the default permissions on upload?

issue_title: merge doesn't work for models related with on_delete=Protect
issue_body:
Hi !

When trying to merge objects that have are related with on_delete=Protect, the merge fails because of a `ProtectedError` exception.

When doing a merge and choosing to move the relations, I think one expects that the relations are moved to the "master" before actually deleting the "other", so I don't think the protection should apply here.
Actually, from what I understood of the code, it's exactly what's happening.

Here's an easy hack: https://github.com/olivierdalang/django-adminactions/commit/9938d1072422db0e524ec8ae70627ad1e3ff307a
Not sure if there's no side-effect, I didn't think it through, and I didn't manage to find out how to write a test for this, since it would need to create custom models (with protected relations), hence no PR.

Thanks for the great work !!

Docs » PayPal Payments Standard » IPN/PDT variables

You provide link for **obsolete PayPal documentation**: https://developer.paypal.com/webapps/developer/docs/classic/ipn/integration-guide/IPNandPDTVariables/

In Django 1.10.5 errors related to obsolete data variables that are not returned on the IPN object are not raised or printed in the console by default. To do this I had to add this code in _my_app.__int__.py_

```
from django.conf import settings

class ExceptionLoggingMiddleware(object):
    def __init__(self, get_response):
        self.get_response = get_response

    def __call__(self, request):
        response = self.get_response(request)
        return response

    def process_exception(self, request, exception):
        if settings.DEBUG:
            print(exception.__class__.__name__)
            return None
```
and also add to project's _settings.py_:
```
MIDDLEWARE = [
    ...
    'my_project.my_app.ExceptionLoggingMiddleware',
]
```

issue_title: Wrong error: SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT must not be None when SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH set to "plaintext"
issue_body:
The auth token retrieval does not work in a testing environment with SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH being set to 'plaintext'. Using 'plaintext' makes sense for running unit tests (the crypt routines otherwise may significantly affect the duration it takes to run the tests).

Consider this small application:
```python
from flask import Flask, render_template, g
from flask.ext.sqlalchemy import SQLAlchemy
import flask.ext.security
from flask.ext.security import UserMixin, RoleMixin
from flask.ext.security.registerable import register_user

auth_required = flask.ext.security.auth_token_required
app = Flask(__name__)
app.config['DEBUG'] = True
app.config['SECURITY_SEND_REGISTER_EMAIL'] = False
app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'a'
app.config['WTF_CSRF_ENABLED'] = False
app.config['SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI'] = "sqlite://"

db = SQLAlchemy(app)

roles_users = db.Table('roles_users',
    db.Column('user_id', db.Integer(), db.ForeignKey('user.id')),
    db.Column('role_id', db.Integer(), db.ForeignKey('role.id')))

class Role(db.Model, RoleMixin):
    id = db.Column(db.Integer(), primary_key=True)
    name = db.Column(db.String(80), unique=True)
    description = db.Column(db.String(255))

class User(db.Model, UserMixin):
    id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True)
    email = db.Column(db.String(255), unique=True)
    password = db.Column(db.String(255))
    active = db.Column(db.Boolean())
    confirmed_at = db.Column(db.DateTime())
    roles = db.relationship(
        'Role',
        secondary=roles_users,
        backref=db.backref('users', lazy='dynamic')
        )

user_datastore = flask.ext.security.SQLAlchemyUserDatastore(db, User, Role)
security = flask.ext.security.Security(app, user_datastore)

@app.route('/createtestuser')
def create_user():
    db.drop_all()
    db.create_all()
    register_user(email="mail1", password="testpassword1")
    return "OK"

app.run(debug=True, host="0.0.0.0")
```

Create a test user:

```
$ curl -i http://localhost:5000/createtestuser                                                                  HTTP/1.0 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
Content-Length: 2
Set-Cookie: session=eyJfaWQiOnsiIGIiOiJNR0ZtTVRjd1lqVTRZMkppTjJFMk0yTm1NMll4TkRKbFltVXpNR0l5WW1FPSJ9fQ.CCu0Ww.dnadUzB93ML3oZt2EdrpbFS5bnA; HttpOnly; Path=/
Server: Werkzeug/0.10.4 Python/2.7.3
Date: Wed, 06 May 2015 14:19:07 GMT

OK
```

Attempt to retrieve auth token:
```
$ curl -i --data '{"password": "testpassword1", "email": "mail1"}' http://localhost:5000/login  -H "Content-Type: application/json"
```
Yields the following traceback:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1836, in __call__
    return self.wsgi_app(environ, start_response)
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1820, in wsgi_app
    response = self.make_response(self.handle_exception(e))
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1403, in handle_exception
    reraise(exc_type, exc_value, tb)
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1817, in wsgi_app
    response = self.full_dispatch_request()
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1477, in full_dispatch_request
    rv = self.handle_user_exception(e)
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1381, in handle_user_exception
    reraise(exc_type, exc_value, tb)
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1475, in full_dispatch_request
    rv = self.dispatch_request()
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1461, in dispatch_request
    return self.view_functions[rule.endpoint](**req.view_args)
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask_security/decorators.py", line 205, in wrapper
    return f(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask_security/views.py", line 79, in login
    if form.validate_on_submit():
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask_wtf/form.py", line 166, in validate_on_submit
    return self.is_submitted() and self.validate()
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask_security/forms.py", line 238, in validate
    if not verify_and_update_password(self.password.data, self.user):
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask_security/utils.py", line 137, in verify_and_update_password
    password = get_hmac(password)
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask_security/utils.py", line 110, in get_hmac
    'set to "%s"' % _security.password_hash)
RuntimeError: The configuration value `SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT` must not be None when the value of `SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH` is set to "plaintext"
```
This error message is contradictory to the documentation which clearly states that SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT "Specifies the HMAC salt. **This is only used if the password hash type is set to something other than plain text**. Defaults to None."

So I set a salt which theoretically could work with the plaintext hashing method:
```
app.config['SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT'] = b"xxx"
```
(I still leave the default for SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH which is 'plaintext'). The traceback disappears, but now the password validation is broken:

```
$ curl -i --data '{"password": "testpassword1", "email": "mail1"}' http://localhost:5000/login  -H "Content-Type: application/json"
HTTP/1.0 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json

    "code": 400
  },
  "response": {
    "errors": {
      "password": [
        "Invalid password"
      ]
    }
  }
}
```
That is, the password validation method does not properly account for this scenario, right?

So I set a hashing method different from plaintext:
```
app.config['SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH'] = "sha512_crypt"
```

Now the password validation works and I retrieve an auth token. But, really, this should work with the default settings (no hash method, no salt), or there should be an improved error message.

As a side note: As you can see above, the response always contains a `Set-Cookie: session=eyJfaWQiOnsiIGIi......` header. Why is that? Is that proper behavior? Does it come from the Flask-Login extension? Its documentation states that it only sets a cookie when using `remember=True` upon executing the login routine. How can I make the minimal app shown above make *not* set a cookie upon each request? Should I open another issue for that?

issue_title: Allow user to pass a custom dump function to export metadata
issue_body:
Based on discussion in #9 with @maxgrenderjones. This would allow a user to pass in a custom function to export metadata in `frontmatter.dump` and `frontmatter.dumps`.

For example, use `pyaml.dump` instead of `yaml.safe_dump`. The big question is how to pass in arguments. If you want safe mode with `pyaml.dump`, how do you pass in `safe=True`.

Potential implementations:

**Use a little wrapper function:**

``` python
>>> from somepackage import dumperfunc
>>> frontmatter.dumps(post, dump_func=lambda x: dumperfunc(x, foo=1, bar=2, quux=3))
```

**Forward `kwargs` to `dump_func`:**

``` python
>>> import pyaml
>>> post = frontmatter.loads(text, title="Hello, World")
>>> frontmatter.dumps(post, dump_func=pyaml.dump, **pyaml_kwargs)
```

And then,

``` python
def dumps(post, dump_func, **kwargs):
   kwargs=kwargs.copy() 
   if dump_func==pyaml.dump: # obvs would need to do this in a way that doesn't rely on pyaml being available
       if 'foo' not in kwargs:
          kwargs['foo']='defaultfoo'
          ..snip snip...

   dump_func(post.metadata, **kwargs)
```

Big question: Does this have to output YAML? What if the function is `json.dumps`?

And is there a sane way to handle default arguments (such as safe mode)?

issue_title: Support for syncing User Defined Types
issue_body: http://datastax.github.io/python-driver/cqlengine/models.html#user-defined-types

Hi

I had my Cassandra API hooked up to docker image python3.6 (stupid me)
And when they updated the docker image my API image got updated and I couln't run migrations to Cassandra anymore. I looked for a long while until I realised that they have changed the import exception in importlib for 3.6.3.

> importlib
> Import now raises the new exception ModuleNotFoundError (subclass of ImportError) when it cannot find a module. Code that current checks for ImportError (in try-except) will still work. (Contributed by Eric Snow in bpo-15767.)

In the code for sync_cassandra is this:

`        for app_name in settings.INSTALLED_APPS:
            try:
                import_module('.management', app_name)
            except SystemError:
                # We get SystemError if INSTALLED_APPS contains the
                # name of a class rather than a module
                pass
            except ImportError as exc:
                # This is slightly hackish. We want to ignore ImportErrors
                # if the "management" module itself is missing -- but we don't
                # want to ignore the exception if the management module exists
                # but raises an ImportError for some reason. The only way we
                # can do this is to check the text of the exception. Note that
                # we're a bit broad in how we check the text, because different
                # Python implementations may not use the same text.
                # CPython uses the text "No module named management"
                # PyPy uses "No module named myproject.myapp.management"
                msg = exc.args[0]
                if not msg.startswith('No module named') \
                        or 'management' not in msg:
                    raise` 

I'm not really sure about the correct way to solve it to keep it backwards compatible. Since I got a problem when defining my apps like myapp.apps.MyappConfig. I should have gotten a Systemerror and nothing would have been done, but now we get a ModuleNotFoundError and everything broke. But shouldn't that been caught with the Import Error since it is derived from that?

If I get some input on how to solve the best I'll make a pull request of the change

issue_title: Allow splitting docs into multiple files
issue_body:
`pydoc-markdown` will currently generate a single markdown formatted text that is output to stdout. This is not practical for large packages. It should be possible to split the documentation into separate files per module/class (configurable).

Note that we need to generate appropriate relative cross-file links hat are interpreted correctly by the markdown parser. BitBucket and GitHub are quite different in that regards, and it also makes a difference whether the markdown is hosted on a GitHub Wiki or in the repository. This all needs to be configurable.

About links, see also #4

issue_title: Should throw an error if you ask for an invalid migration state.
issue_body:
If I ask for a migration state that is disallowed, should error, rather than carrying on and getting it wrong.

Eg, if app1 has two migrations, 0001, 0002
And app2 has a migration 0001, that depends on app1.0002

If I set `before = [('app1', '0001'), ('app2', '0002')]` then I am asking for something that cannot happen.

What will happen at present is that we just run the migrate commands sequentially. In affect:
```
django migrate app1 0001
django migrate app2 0002
```

Which will just end us up with app1 being at 0002, which is not what was asked for. This will require some digging around, because django doesn't (I believe) let you specify multiply targets when migrating.

issue_title: hashivault_write with update tries to write empty string to unset values
issue_body:
Maybe this is not a problem in most cases, but for some secrets it matters.
E.g. here:

```
- hashivault_write:
    update: true
    secret: "/auth/aws/role/myrole"
    data:
      auth_type: ec2
      policies:
      - mypolicy
      disallow_reauthentication: true
      bound_iam_instance_profile_arn: "arn:aws:iam::1234:instance-profile/myinstanceprofile"
```

This works if the secret is new but if it already exists and I've changed the `bound_iam_instance_profile_arn` value then it fails with
```
"Exception: failed updating the unique ID of ARN \"\": &errors.errorString{s:\"unrecognized arn: contains 1 colon-separated parts, expected 6\"}"
```

My understanding is that this is because it tries to write empty string to the other arn-field (`bound_iam_principal_arn`)

issue_title: Exclude from Meta is not working when you use an explicit serializer
issue_body:
```
class Item(Document):
    name = StringField(max_length=255, required=False)
    units = ListField(ReferenceField(Unit))

class BasicUnitSerializer(DocumentSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Unit
        fields = ('unit_id', 'name', )
        depth = 1
        verbose = 'unit'
        verbose_plural = 'units'

class ItemSerializer(DocumentSerializer):
    units = BasicUnitSerializer(many=True, required=False)
    class Meta:
        model = Item
        exclude = ('units', )
        verbose_plural = 'items'
        depth = 1
```

If I use BasicUnitSerializer for units, the exclude does not work. But if I let units be serialized by a default NestedSerializer, then the exclude works.
Unlike 'django-filters', the drfm-filters make no sense without drfm.

Suppose that you have a Document with EmbeddedDocument within:

```
class Child(EmbeddedDocument):
    name = StringField()

class Parent(Document):
    child = EmbeddedDocumentField(Child)
```

and you want to add a custom validator for the name field. This is possible with DRF, but not with DRF-ME currently.

I want to add a special syntax for `DocumentSerializer` to validate fields on its `EmbeddedDocumentSerializer`. Declaring a method `validate_child__name()` on `DocumentSerializer` will make it apply this method to its `EmbeddedDocumentSerializer`'s `name` field:

```
class Parent(Document):
    child = EmbeddedDocumentField(Child)

    def validate_child__name()
```

Note that (like in rest of Django) double underscore replaces dot in method name (`child.name` -> `child__name`).

issue_body: The documentation for MNIST EC2 example states that we can set the `NUM_GPU` environment variable to specify how many GPUs we want to be used per node. Is the same true for Hadoop based YARN clusters as well?

see:
https://blog.logrocket.com/visualizing-backend-performance-in-the-chrome-devtools-bb6fd232540

issue_title: LSTM doesn't work with Symbols - "'Symbol' object has no attribute 'shape'"
issue_body:
I am using the following versions,
```
$ pip list | grep mxnet
mxnet (1.0.0.post1)
$ python --version
Python 3.6.3
```

When I run,
```python
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import gluon

class Net(gluon.Block):
    def __init__(self, recurrent_width, forward_width, **kwargs):
        super(Net, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        with self.name_scope():
            self._rnn = gluon.rnn.LSTM(recurrent_width)
            self._nn  = gluon.nn.Dense(forward_width)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self._nn(self._rnn(x))

net = Net(10, 10)
net(mx.sym.var('data'))
```

I get,
```
$ python shape_error.py 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "shape_error.py", line 15, in <module>
    net(mx.sym.var('data'))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/mxnet/gluon/block.py", line 304, in __call__
    return self.forward(*args)
  File "shape_error.py", line 12, in forward
    return self._nn(self._rnn(x))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/mxnet/gluon/block.py", line 304, in __call__
    return self.forward(*args)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/mxnet/gluon/rnn/rnn_layer.py", line 173, in forward
    batch_size = inputs.shape[self._layout.find('N')]
AttributeError: 'Symbol' object has no attribute 'shape'
```

So the problem is occuring [here](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/gluon/rnn/rnn_layer.py#L173) because RNN Layer checks the shape right away.

How can I fix this?

issue_title: Logging in Mxnet, with different level (verbose, warning, error)
issue_body:
Hi,

As pointed out in this forum thread, https://discuss.mxnet.io/t/how-to-enable-logginig-for-c-api/1273/5 and https://discuss.mxnet.io/t/debug-mxnet-network-in-c/1274, there is a strong need felt to be able to log information and failed checks in MXNet.
It is mainly useful when using the C++ API, because there are no other feedback than the one you get when there is an error and than you need to dive deep in the code to finally get what went wrong. (For example, that the expected input shape of the symbol is not consistent to the one that has been gotten).

Moreover, a consistent way to get the logging information would be great. Meaning, one that doesn't need to modify environment variables and so on, would be appreciated. It would then be possible to have several level of logging, according on what is common in most of the projects out there, including "verbose, debug, info, warning, error".
Also, depending on the level of information gathered, having all the log output in file would be appreciated.

Thanks!
